title: Chapter 36
text: Chapter Thirty- Three T he lights of Cheyenne — so out of place in the pitch-black wasteland that had once been Wyoming — illuminated a swath of the overcast night sky. Ronin recognized the truth of the town, now; Cheyenne wasn’t a beacon of hope and security, but a monument to Warlord’s power and prejudice. The light was visible from miles around, projecting the challenge — I have what you do not; try to take from me so I may destroy you and claim what was yours , too . Outwardly, it was an impregnable fortress, a stronghold none could stand against. Ronin had single-handedly ended scores of reavers out in the Dust; surely, Warlord’s gearheads could handle even more from so defensible a position . But, despite its imposing appearance, Warlord’s wall was ineffective. Alpha Team, led by Ronin and Sergeant Maul, had crossed the nothingness between the base and Cheyenne and walked directly into the wall’s shadow, unopposed. The irregularly-placed guard towers on the north wall were empty. Ronin couldn’t guess what Warlord had tasked his gearheads with to leave their security so lax, but it didn’t matter . If everything went as planned, all the gearheads would be drawn out soon enough . Ronin stood beside Maul in the darkness beneath the wall, optics straying southwest. Somewhere, just under a mile-and-a-half distant, Lara and Bravo Team were arriving on the outskirts of the slums. Though Alpha Team was larger, Captain Cooper and some of his most experienced soldiers were with Lara . “Time to get our sorry asses over this wall,” Maul said, slinging his rifle across his back. His was amongst the first faces Ronin had seen after arriving at the base — Maul had been one of the soldiers who escorted Ronin and Newton inside . The other bots, Ronin included, swung their weapons free of their hands and stepped up to the wall. The human soldiers formed a perimeter, some of them equipped with old night vision goggles . “Land gracefully, boys,” one of the humans whispered. A quiet snicker spread through the team . “Break a leg, fellas,” a bot replied . “Shut up.” Maul swept his optics over the group until they were silent. “I’m not in the mood for any new ventilation ports.” Turning back to the wall, he directed one of the synths to boost him up. Most of the bots could jump high enough to haul themselves over the ten-foot-tall barrier, but it would be too noisy . The last time Ronin crossed the wall, he’d been running. He thought he’d known true fear that day, that his concern for Lara had reached its peak, but the panic of their flight seemed so small and insignificant compared to what they faced afterward. Her death had always been a possibility, but he’d never acknowledged it. Not really . Before they left Cheyenne, he’d promised to keep her safe, and hadn’t doubted his ability to do so regardless of the situation. It had only taken a few days to fail . With the assistance of another bot, Ronin pulled himself smoothly up onto the wall; out of the darkness and into the artificial light. The Clinic loomed ahead, dark from this angle except for the street lamps on the roads surrounding it. The land around it was covered in mostly-green, neatly cropped grass — a lawn. A line of fir trees separated the grounds from the rest of the bot district, though bits of the houses beyond were visible through the boughs . He swung his gaze southwest again, longing for even a glimpse of the ramshackle settlement beyond the wall. If he ran, he could reach her in minutes, could be with his Lara and know she was safe . Instead, he picked his way down through the bent lengths of steel, splintered boards, and cracked chunks of concrete protruding from the wall’s inside face. Perhaps it was meant as much to keep people in as it was to keep them out . Ronin swung his rifle into his hands — it was solid and well-maintained, but its feel was unfamiliar — and joined and the others who’d already crossed the wall. They kneeled along the rear of the building, expanding their perimeter as more men trickled over . Within two minutes, the entirety of Alpha Team was crouched in the grass beside the Clinic . “Place is quiet,” someone remarked . “Shh. Listen .” The wind sighed through the trees and grass, and, more distantly, it howled over the vast, indifferent Dust. Closer, the Clinic’s electric lights hummed. Nearly lost amidst those sounds were two distinct voices, engaged in conversation. Ronin’s audio receptors isolated them, picking the words out from the background noise . “My processors keep going around in circles. Going to wind up with a critical error at some point,” said the first . “I don’t understand why,” replied the other, crackling faintly with static that had nothing to do with the wind. “We’ve seen people come in from the Dust. Logic dictates that there are more settlements out there .” “Logic dictates that even this place shouldn’t exist .” “That logic is flawed. You run diagnostics lately? Your data might be corrupted .” “That’s the whole problem. Logic dictates this place should not exist, yet here it is. If it’s here, there must be other places. But none of those places should exist, either. It’s going to fry my damned CPU if I don’t break the logic chain .” “Why are we talking about this again, anyway? We’ve had the same conversation every time we’re stationed at this post together .” “Because this post is boring enough that I worry my joints will rust up and I’ll be stuck in this spot forever,” the first replied . “Another irrational thought process. We would — ” Ronin cut his audio receptors back to his immediate vicinity. He recognized the first voice; it belonged to Reg, the synth who normally guarded the east road . “At least two gearheads, guarding the main entrance,” Ronin said . “We can take one of these other doors,” someone offered. “There’s at least four that we can see from here .” “No idea if they’re barricaded from within, or just locked,” Ronin responded. “And my mapping only covers the active portion of the building. Maybe fifteen percent of the complex, at best .” “We’re going to have to deal with all of them, at some point,” Maul said . “Would be ideal if we had full control of the Clinic before we attracted attention.” Ronin ran his optics over their surroundings again. Everything was relatively still, relatively quiet. That only meant any sort of alarm would be heard all-too-clearly by the bots deeper in the district . “Yeah. So, we need to make this as quick and quiet as possible. Dodge and Dozer, you’re with me and Ronin. Time to introduce ourselves to these gearheads .” Two synths stepped forward. One was a slight female with short blonde hair. DOZER was painted across the front of her helmet in thick black letters. Ronin’s data contained information on large, powerful, pre-Black Out machines called bulldozers, but it was fragmented . “At some point before the Memory Wipe, someone modified her,” Maul explained. “She’s not retrofitted for combat, like a lot of us were, but her actuators are the most advanced we’ve seen. Might’ve been some experimental shit .” Dozer offered Ronin a lopsided grin and fell into place behind Maul . The Sergeant gestured to the other soldiers. “Gracie, Eisener, Palitto, and Morrison. Flank around the east side. Keep below the windows, and stick to the shadows. I want you in position and ready to open fire if necessary .” The group hurried off, disappearing around an outside corner . “Rest of you, hold tight. Ears on for a signal to advance .” Ronin followed Maul, their footfalls hushed over the soft grass . Were Lara and her team in position yet? Was she safe? For several seconds, the urge to change course threatened to override his current processes . Somehow, he resisted, crossing the lawn to the Clinic’s brick face. He readied his rifle, missing the familiar grooves worn into the grip of his old firearm. His prior weapon had served him reliably for fifty-seven years. The attachment to it was irrational, but he couldn’t deny it . Maul halted at the corner, pressed his back against the wall, and peered around it. Ronin knew the layout well. This section of the building ran north-south. Around the corner, another portion ran perpendicular to this one. The front doors were at their meeting point . Ronin ran his optics over the area. The expanse of short-cropped grass, bordered on one side by a neat row of manicured trees, did not belong in this world — at least not under a leader like Warlord . Though he possessed no memory of seeing them in use, Ronin understood Maul’s hand signals . Two enemies. Armed . “Eighty-five meters between us and that door. And they’ll have optics on us the moment we leave cover .” Ronin’s processors ramped up, tumbling through thousands of simulated approaches. Leaving cover was a necessity, but only opening fire on the guard bots immediately afterward had a high likelihood of success . The consequences of that action, however … He stepped away from the wall and unslung his rifle. Grasping it by the barrel, he held it out to Maul . “The hell are you doing?” the Sergeant asked . “Taking the most direct approach.” Ronin gestured to Dozer. “Give your weapon to Dodge .” “You must be in the throes of a critical error,” she replied, stone- faced . “Gunfire will draw attention. We don’t want that yet, not until we’re ready .” “So, the answer is to walk up to them and ask if we can go in?” Maul asked . “No. She’s going to walk up to them, dragging me along,” Ronin said . “And what will that accomplish, besides giving them some easy targets?” Dozer held her rifle across her chest, casually resting her left hand on the barrel. It was an incredibly human pose . “This is the Clinic. The place all the bots in Cheyenne come to for repairs. They see you dragging another bot with limited mobility up to the front doors, and their reaction will not be to shoot .” “I did some of the recon on this place,” she said, “and I’ve seen what some of them are packing. We’re asking to be scrapped, walking up there unarmed .” “From what the Sergeant said, it won’t matter what they’re packing if we can get you within arm’s reach .” She raised a brow, and one corner of her mouth scrunched. Skeptical, but intrigued? How much had these bots picked up from humans, living in the base for so long ? Maul leaned forward, taking another glance around the corner. He raised a fist before turning back to Ronin. “The other team is in position. Anything goes wrong, the two of you go prone, and we’ll fire from both angles. It’ll be noisy, but we’re not losing anyone just to take out these two bastards .” Dozer was silent and unmoving. Seconds ticked by on Ronin’s internal clock; to the east, pre-dawn light was touching the perpetually hazy sky. Bravo team had to be moving through the humans’ shacks now, guarding Lara as she roused her people. Warlord’s forces needed to be occupied before the humans amassed in the market. Otherwise, the people would be slaughtered . Finally, the female bot stirred. Without a word, she lifted the strap of her rifle over her head and handed the weapon to Dodge. Stepping forward, she glanced up at Ronin expectantly, spreading her open hands to either side . Ronin moved alongside her, turning to face the same direction, and slung an arm over her shoulders. When she wrapped hers around his waist, Lara’s face flashed up from his memory. He didn’t want to be so near to anyone but her. Despite the gravity of the situation, he wondered how she’d feel about the contact between himself and Dozer; would she be hurt or jealous? Why did his processors, against all logic, tell him this was wrong ? He eased his weight onto Dozer. She dipped slightly, for only a moment, before her actuators adjusted to compensate . “Heavy SOB. You retrofitted with a reinforced casing?” she asked . “ Apparently .” “Good. This goes wrong, I’m using you as a bullet shield .” He overrode the normal functioning of his actuators, locking his left knee and fully loosening the joints in his right. As he sagged forward, hanging his head to hide his face, Dozer clasped his wrist and tailored her posture to spread his weight . “Ready?” she asked . “ Yes .” She walked forward. Ronin’s legs dragged over the concrete. They rounded the corner, stepping onto the walkway. He kept his head down, optics on her boots . “What’s this?” the static-voiced gearhead demanded. Eighty-one meters to go . “He needs repairs,” Dozer answered. Seventy- three . “Haven’t seen you around before .” “Don’t have any record of your face on file,” Reg said . “We’re dustwalkers,” Ronin offered without raising his head, altering his voice modulation. “Just got into town. I took a spill in a ditch outside the wall .” “A ditch, or a canyon?” Reg asked. Fifty-seven meters . “His optics have been failing for years, and the last place he was repaired didn’t do a very good job,” Dozer said. Thirty-eight meters . “Brought some scrap in, and we were told this place provides the best repairs this side of the Dust,” Ronin explained. Variables fluttered through his processors, spiraling into countless scenarios of wildly fluctuating probability. They needed to get close, needed to seize the Clinic, needed to get to Lara and keep her safe . Dozer stopped, swaying slightly beneath Ronin’s weight. The wind whipped around the building, tugging at Ronin’s clothes. Thirty meters. Dozer’s actuators moved audibly within her casing . Electric crackled over Ronin’s cheek. He locked his arm to prevent himself from scratching . “They couldn’t be here if they weren’t admitted,” Reg reasoned . “Yes. You’re right,” static bot replied . Dozer resumed her walk. Her boots fell heavy on the concrete, which was run through with hastily-repaired cracks . Twenty-five meters. Fifteen. Seven . “How far have you been dragging him?” static bot asked . “Too damned far.” Three meters. “You mind helping me before I blow a motor ?” The gearheads came into view from their abdomens down; their pants were patched but clean, their boots weather-worn but sturdy. Their weapons — two ancient-looking assault rifles with cracked wooden stocks — swung through Ronin’s vision as they stepped forward. Slinging the rifles aside, they took hold of Ronin’s arms and lifted him from Dozer. Static bot slung Ronin’s arm over his shoulders . On the edge of Ronin’s optical field, Dozer lurched forward, grabbing the Reg by the throat. Reverting his knees to normal function, Ronin wrapped his arm fully around static bot’s neck, clasped his wrist with his opposite hand, and squeezed . A peal of static rose from the bot’s voice modulator as it was crushed. Its head drooped to the side, the internal support structure of its neck shattered. Ronin shifted his hold on it, tearing through the back of the bot’s shirt and the synthetic skin beneath. He jammed his fingers beneath the lip of the exposed panel and tore off the cover plate . Static bot struggled, fully aware of what was happening — crippled, but functional. It slammed its flailing arms back into Ronin, who raised his own arm to protect his head as he gripped the energy cell and ripped it out of the compartment . Stilling abruptly, the bot’s arms and head sagged, though it remained standing. Ronin turned to Dozer . She stood over Reg’s prone form. His back casing had been torn open as easily as Ronin had torn static bot’s shirt, the metal shielding bent to the point of breaking at the folds. Cables and wires jutted from his neck; his head, with Warlord’s symbol in blood red on the exposed undercasing, lay ten feet away . Dozer met Ronin’s gaze and tossed the power cell onto the motionless bot at her feet . “Didn’t even get to — ” Ronin’s attention shifted before she finished, moving to the Clinic’s entrance. A figure approached the doors from within, features obscured by the reflections of the outside lights. The synth who worked at the desk, most likely, coming to investigate the commotion. But some hidden process, likely developed over so many years of surviving the Dust, told him it wasn’t Mercy . He dropped to a knee, taking hold of static bot’s rifle and lifting it quickly enough that its strap broke off at the barrel. Grasping the foregrip, he pulled back the bolt handle to ensure a round was chambered and raised the stock to his shoulder as the doors slid open . The only thing familiar about the bot who stepped out was the symbol painted on its chest casing . Microseconds ticked by. Twisting toward Dozer, the gearhead fired his rifle from the hip. The first shot was thunderous in the relative silence . Ronin squeezed the trigger as a second round burst from the gearhead’s rifle. Ronin’s shot punched through the bot’s cranial casing, snapping is head to the side. His actuators adjusted to the recoil, sending the next bullet through the gearhead’s left optical receptor . Sparks spraying from its damaged casing, the gearhead spun to face Ronin . Gunshots rang out from the east and west as Ronin fired three more rounds into the gearhead’s torso; nine holes opened up on it in total. It staggered backward, shattering the front doors. One of its legs gave out and it slumped to the ground . Dozer leapt forward. Bracing a foot against the gearhead’s chest, she grabbed the wrist of its gun arm and tore the entire limb off. She tossed it aside — weapon still in its clutch — and lifted her foot. Her boot slammed back down hard, collapsing the gearhead’s torso . With an electric pop, the gearhead stilled . “Shit,” Dozer muttered . The other soldiers moved forward from the corners, footfalls loud over the concrete . Ronin stood up, rifle secure against his shoulder, and approached Dozer. As he passed the entrance, he saw the synth nurse, Mercy, standing just beyond the interior doors . “How are you holding up, Dozer?” he asked, weapon directed at the Clinic . “Clipped a power conduit,” she replied. “Got a power leak. Could be worse .” “We can repair that here.” Mercy didn’t move from her spot, and didn’t look away from Ronin . “Are there any more of them in there?” Ronin scanned the reception area behind her, but detected no movement . “No. He was the only one who came in for repairs last night. There will be a lot more of them here soon, though .” “That’s the plan,” Ronin said. He glanced over his shoulder as Maul and Dodge arrived, the second fire team close behind them. They all held their weapons at the ready . “Situation?” Maul stepped over the deactivated bot in the entryway, boots crunching on broken glass, and handed Ronin his rifle from the base . “Gonna need a repair later, Sarge, but she says the place is clear,” Dozer replied . “We’re not going to have much time,” Ronin said. He slung the base rifle over his shoulder, keeping the weapon he’d picked up from the gearhead. It would do until it was dry. He approached Mercy, lowering the barrel . “You understand what you’re doing?” she asked. “The potential cost of it ?” He nodded and glanced over his shoulder as the remainder of Alpha team approached. Gently, Ronin placed a hand on Mercy’s arm and guided her aside. Together, they watched the soldiers file into the reception area. The space — which had at one time seemed overly large, given the limited staff and functionality of the facility — was soon overcrowded. The temperature increased three degrees . In Cheyenne, an alarm wailed, echoing through the early morning air . “We need to move fast, people,” Maul said. “Defensive positions, weapons free. We don’t know how many are going to come or what they’ll hit us with, but we will not let them through these doors. Understood ?” This wasn’t a low-risk patrol around the base or a scrap run that might encounter a few reavers . This was war . The soldiers replied in unison, voices strong. “Yes, Sergeant !” “Which way to the stairs?” Ronin asked . Mercy pointed to the west of the entrance. “Some places are cluttered on the upper floors. Old equipment and furnishings, mostly .” “Go back into the repair room. Should be deep enough inside to be safe from any gunfire .” She nodded, lips dropping into a frown. She touched Ronin’s shoulder lightly. “Be careful. He’s won’t stop until you’re all deactivated, or he’s just a heap of spare parts . Ronin watched her walk down the pristine hallway for several seconds before he returned his attention to Maul. Most of the soldiers had dispersed to more advantageous positions. A small group of them remained, moving the seats and desk to create a makeshift barrier across the entry . The siren blared again . Was Lara all right? He hadn’t heard any other shots; shouldn’t that be of some comfort ? “Where do you want me, Sergeant?” Ronin asked . “A window on one of the upper floors. We’ll hold this room here, but if you’re half as quick as you seem to be, I don’t think any of them will make it this far .” “Here’s hoping .” As Maul and another synth pushed a row of heavy, connected chairs into place, Ronin found the stairs. He took them three at a time, up to the fourth floor . Dozer and two human soldiers — Jensen and Ramirez — were already in position. The humans stood at two narrow windows, with Dozer at the large, circular window in between them. Ronin ran his optics over the objects piled in the room — filing cabinets, chairs, old desks, and cardboard boxes that looked like they would crumble at the slightest touch, all blanketed in a thick layer of dust. Leaning up against some of it was a long, weatherworn sign, words faded but legible . The Price of Freedom is Visible Here . It seemed fitting, but how high would that price be ? He moved to the round window, pressing his shoulder to the wall opposite Dozer. The grounds below remained quiet; grass and trees swayed in the wind, and dawn light steadily crept in from the east. Beyond, the many residences sat peacefully, indifferent to the inevitable conflict that would soon tear Cheyenne apart . From this vantage, the human slums were visible beyond the wall, relatively dark compared to the rest of the town. His hope to spot Lara was a foolish one, but he clung to it regardless. She was out there, recently recovered from serious injuries, risking her life for all the people — organic and metal — in Cheyenne . Most of whom had never done anything for her . “Still doing okay?” he asked Dozer . “As long as we’re done by lunch time, I’ll be fine .” “I’m sorry — ” “No. You’re the reason I don’t have more holes in my casing. I’d look like Swiss cheese otherwise .” “Swiss cheese?” Ronin’s processors poured over decades of data. “I understand that, though I don’t recall having ever seen it .” “Yeah, same here. Weird, the stuff that lingers even after the Memory Wipe. Can’t remember if I had a name, or relationships…or anything else .” “But we hold onto a cheese full of holes,” he said, optics trained on the grounds below. “It’s for the best, most likely. What good would it do for us to remember a dead world? Better to connect to the world we have and build it anew as best we can .” “Old world must not have been too great anyway,” said Jensen, “else it wouldn’t have ended like it did .” “I could imagine a few better things than living and dying in a concrete hole,” Ramirez replied. “Maybe one of those beaches, like in Anderson’s old vids .” “Guess there used to be some pretty tall buildings, too. Like, even taller than this one.” Jensen’s skepticism was clear in his voice . “Everything was bigger, back then,” Dozer said, staring out at the grounds . “Big enough to make this mess when it all came down.” A twitch crackled over Ronin’s cheek, but he ignored it. The old world had exploded, and this one was harsh and unforgiving, but he had Lara here. He didn’t need skyscrapers or beaches or automobiles or holey cheese; she was all he required in this world or any other . The chain of probabilities — the ‘what-ifs’ — spiraled on into infinity, daunting even to his processors. So many things could have gone differently, so many variables might have resulted in a world without his fiery Lara Brooks . His optics detected movement on the road, beyond the trees . Gearheads. Some of their marks were visible through the gaps between the boughs. At least half a dozen of them approaching from the east and three or four more from the west. The two groups combined and crossed the field, heading north — directly toward the front entrance. Due to the trees at the edge of the lawn, Ronin didn’t have a clear line-of-sight, but they would have to break from the cover to cross the circular driveway below the windows . “Get ready, boys,” Dozer said flatly . Ramirez released a shaky breath . The gearheads — ten in total — emerged from the trees and hurried along the cracked pavement, toward the patch of grass at the center of the drive. One hundred and fifty feet…one hundred and forty … They slowed, undoubtedly spotting their deactivated comrades and the barricaded front doors . “Now!” Dozer called . She and Ronin shattered the window and leveled their weapons. He selected a target — the gearhead called Northside — and opened fire. More rifles went off to either side and on the lower levels. Bullets rained down on the gearheads, punching holes in their casings, ricocheting to cut grooves in the grass and dirt. One gearhead went down almost immediately, limbs shaking and bending at unnatural angles . The others, damaged but not incapacitated, returned fire. Bullets cracked into the face of the building, breaking the ancient bricks into dust. Glass shattered, and the gunshots came so quickly that they were almost indistinguishable from one another. Ronin fired into Northside as the gearheads retreated toward the trees . Calculating Northside’s movement, Ronin squeezed the trigger again. The rifle bucked, and five-hundredths of a second later, the bullet tore through the gearhead’s chest. Blue fire sprayed from its back and eyes, and it fell to the pavement, smoking . Several of the remaining gearheads fired bursts up at the fourth floor. Cracking brick and splintering wood dominated Ronin’s audio receptors as the rounds ripped through the wall, spraying debris across the room. Several rounds connected with his casing. Only two breached — a partial penetration on his right thigh and a full one over his left hip. Diagnostics reported mobility loss due to the damage, flashing alerts across his system. He dismissed them; such damage could be addressed later . Dozer released a frustrated growl and turned sharply away from the window, lifting a hand to her face. Shrapnel had damaged her left optic. Muttering another curse, she raised her rifle and fired a burst out the window . Ronin returned to his position, shifting his weapon to his left hand to compensate for his inability to twist his hips beyond a few degrees . The gearheads scrambled beyond the tree line. Bullets caught the trunks, shredding wood, and kicked up clods of dirt and grass below. The gearheads fired blindly from behind their cover. On the road, more figures approached . They weren’t friendly reinforcements. No, it was too early for that, and it was probably too much to hope for, anyway. Warlord owned most of the guns in Cheyenne . “Won’t be long before they try to flank us,” Ronin said, “if they haven’t already .” Dozer’s gaze was grim and unwavering. “I haven’t used you as a bullet shield yet. Maybe that’ll be my chance .” Below, at least twelve more gearheads joined their eight damaged comrades. With both sides of the conflict under some sort of cover, a hail of bullets would only waste ammunition. Alpha Team’s supply wouldn’t last through a day-long firefight. How many bullets had Warlord stockpiled ? “ Fuck .” Ronin turned his head to Ramirez. Pale-faced, the soldier gritted his teeth and pressed a hand to his abdomen. Blood oozed over it . “That doesn’t look good,” Dozer said quietly. She fired off three more rounds in quick succession . Ramirez leaned back into the wall and slid down it onto the floor, leaving a streak of blood behind him . Ronin and Jensen rushed to the wounded soldier, kneeling on either side of him . “Shit, Ramirez…shit! Okay. We got McGowan downstairs, he can patch you up until we get you to the Doc.” Jensen scrambled to his feet. “I’ll get him, and you’ll be okay . Just — ” Ronin grabbed Jensen’s sleeve, halting him . “What, man? We gotta get help for him !” Leaning forward, Ronin met Ramirez’s gaze. The youth’s breathing was shallow. Sweat rolled down his face from beneath his helmet . “Fuck, it hurts,” Ramirez said through his teeth . “I know,” Ronin replied. “Just focus on me and breathe. You can’t stay here, Ramirez .” “No, no. I can’t walk.” Ramirez squeezed his eyes shut. He shook his head with weak desperation. “Hurts too much .” “It’s going to hurt more soon, but you need to stay with me. The next bullet that comes through this wall will kill you if we don’t move .” “We shouldn’t move him.” Jensen rubbed a hand over his face, smearing dirt across his cheek . No one came out of the Dust clean. No one . More gunshots boomed outside. Ronin laid the ancient rifle on the floor. “Help is downstairs .” Ramirez shook his head again. He didn’t open his eyes, didn’t slow his breathing . Ronin’s processors raced, running through a myriad of possibilities. Most of their options ended with the young soldier’s probable death. But Ronin’s memory kept turning to the dark rooms downstairs. Rooms that were adapted for human care. If any of that equipment was still functional … Ronin slipped one arm beneath Ramirez’s legs and the other around his back. Something ground and stuttered in his hip. The soldier screamed, clutching at Ronin with one hand, keeping the other pressed over his gut. Ronin stood . Jensen muttered curses, pacing restlessly . “Jensen, get your ass to a window and return fire!” Dozer shouted . After a brief hesitation, Jensen obeyed, throwing his shoulder against the wall beside Ramirez’s narrow window. His rifle roared — its thunder matched by Ramirez’s screams — while Ronin carried the wounded soldier into the stairwell . Ramirez quieted as they moved down . “Still with me, Ramirez?” Ronin asked, adjusting his suspension systems to keep the wounded soldier as still as possible . “ Sorry …” “For what?” They passed the doorway to the third floor . “What I said about your girl,” Ramirez replied, his voice strained. “Wasn’t right .” Ronin’s hip locked, causing his foot to come down hard on the second floor landing . “Fuck.” Ramirez clenched his jaw “I promise that wasn’t retaliation,” Ronin said, without humor . They emerged on the ground floor. The soldiers in the reception room shouted to one another over the booming rifles, the noise amplified as it echoed down the halls. Ronin carefully set Ramirez on the floor at the rear of the room and maxed his vocalizer, calling for McGowan . The medic was an older model bot — built in humanoid shape, but not styled to mimic a human’s appearance. His casing was dull gray and sleek, with gangly limbs. He broke away from his comrades and moved across the room with his body folded in an awkward crouch . McGowan’s metal legs clacked on the floor as he sank down beside Ramirez. “Samuel, I need you to remove your hand so I can assess your injury .” “Told you…not to fucking…call me that .” “J-just move i-i-it,” McGowan replied, his optics — two large, reflective circles — visibly adjusting. With gentle firmness, he pried Ramirez’s hand away. Fresh blood rose from the wound, visible through the tear in the man’s shirt. Head swiveling on a neck with too many joints, McGowan delicately prodded the entry wound, and then guided Ramirez to sit up so he could examine the exit wound. “Prognosis omitted .” “The fuck that mean?” Ramirez demanded . “High likelihood of perforations to internal organs and presence of contaminated foreign material. Emergency surgery unviable at current location and/or situation .” “There are rooms in this facility that were likely set up for the care of humans. If any of those worked …” McGowan’s optics contracted and dilated. His head trembled faintly for just under a second. “I n-need access,” he finally said . Ronin nodded and stood up. Despite his damaged hip, he ran through the halls, following his memory to the repair room. Ramirez’s condition flashed repeatedly through his CPU, churning up more memories . Memories of Lara battered, bloodied, and broken . Mercy was in the repair room, along with two other synths Ronin hadn’t seen — a male and a female — and the machine attendant. They all turned sharply toward the door at Ronin’s entry . “Is it finished?” Mercy asked . “No, but we have at least one wounded human. Is there anything in this place that could help ?” “Despite their disuse, the operating rooms have been maintained. There is a functioning Automated Operating Table in one of them,” the attendant replied . “I’m not asking any of you to fight,” Ronin said, “but we need your assistance. If you want Warlord deposed...all of us have parts to play .” They stared at Ronin in silence for several seconds, undoubtedly weighing chances of success against risks and rewards. Finally, Mercy stood up . “I believe there’s enough uncorrupted data in my memory to help,” she said. “Take me to the patient .” All four bots followed Ronin. The gunfire’s volume increased drastically as they neared the reception area. Ramirez clung to McGowan, and the bot had his hands clasped over the entry and exit wounds, blood smeared over his casing . “We have more wounded incoming,” McGowan said when Ronin arrived . “They’re going to help.” Ronin indicated the other bots. “Coordinate with them .” Leaving the group to their work, Ronin moved forward, dipping into a crouch to remain behind the cover. The barricade had dozens of holes through it. He kneeled beside Maul as another volley of bullets hammered the barrier. Bits of plastic, wood, and metal fell over the soldiers . “We’ve knocked out at least six of them,” Maul said, voice modulator turned up to overcome the cacophony, “but we count another twenty-plus still functioning on top of that .” “A lot of those are damaged,” Ronin said. “We just need to hold out. Do we have anyone monitoring our flanks?” He shifted his rifle into his hands . “What should I have them watch? There are a hundred damned windows for them to come through. They could get in from anywhere .” The gearheads’ gunfire shifted focus to the upper floors. Ronin, Maul, and the others rose, looking over the barricade. Ronin’s optics detected numerous targets; he took shots in quick succession. To both sides, the soldiers fired as well . A gearhead stumbled out from behind a tree, one arm dangling limply at its side, clothing and casing full of holes. Within half a second, a dozen more rounds perforated the bot, stilling it completely . Movement farther back caught Ronin’s attention. He refocused, and for an instant, all his processes ceased. Even before the videos at the base, he’d been all-too-familiar with the unassuming, unremarkable face staring back at him. The skin over Warlord’s jaw had been replaced, though there was still a scar on his cheek. It was longer and at a sharper angle, the sutures spread further apart, but it wasn’t the scar that made Warlord . It was the emptiness in his optics . Ronin pivoted to aim, damaged hip grinding. Warlord didn’t move as the dustwalker squeezed the trigger . A massive bot stepped in front of Warlord. The bullet hit its broad, blocky chest and ricocheted into the ground, leaving only a faint scratch on its olive-drab casing . “The hell is that?” someone asked . “Compactor,” Ronin replied. The door guard from Kitty’s . Comp loped forward, tearing up chunks of dirt and grass in its wake, charging directly for the front entrance. More rounds bounced off its armored casing. The shots didn’t slow its momentum at all. Gearheads lined up behind it, using it as mobile cover for their advance . “Time to break out the big guns, Sarge,” Ronin said .
